title: Selective Search Algorithm
concept: Selective Search is a classical computer vision algorithm that generates region proposals by grouping pixels based on color, texture, size, and shape compatibility.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does class-agnostic mean in region proposals?

options.text: The algorithm finds regions without knowing what specific object class is inside

exercises.explain: Class-agnostic means identifying potential object locations regardless of their category label.

cards.title: Merging Puzzle Pieces
cards.body: If you look at a puzzle, you start by grouping pieces that have the same color (like sky-blue or grass-green). As you connect them, they form larger structures. The Selective Search algorithm groups pixels in an image using this exact concept.

cards.title: Selective Search
cards.body: Selective Search is a classical (non-neural) computer vision algorithm. It segments the image into small initial regions, then hierarchically merges adjacent regions based on similarities in color, texture, size, and fill compatibility.

cards.title: Lego Structures
cards.body: Imagine a pile of Lego bricks. You start by snapping together bricks of the same color. Soon, small clusters become walls, and walls become houses. Selective Search merges small pixel clusters into larger object proposals.

cards.title: Grouping Cues
cards.body: Selective Search uses multiple cues: Color Similarity (are they both green?), Texture Similarity (do they both have wood grain?), and Size Compatibility (merging smaller regions first to prevent one massive region from swallowing everything).

cards.title: Not a neural network
cards.body: It's a myth that Selective Search uses deep learning or backpropagation. It is a classical, deterministic algorithm with fixed mathematical rules designed to run on a CPU, not a GPU.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a metric used by Selective Search to merge regions?
exercises.explain: Selective Search is a classical algorithm that does not use neural network weights; it relies entirely on pixel-based heuristics.

options.text: The neural network weight values of the backbone

exercises.prompt: True or False: Selective Search begins by dividing the image into a few large blocks and splitting them down.
exercises.explain: False. Selective Search is a bottom-up algorithm: it starts with tiny over-segmented pixel regions and merges them upward into larger regions.
Since it does not use neural network matrices, Selective Search is executed on the CPU.
exercises.sentence: Selective Search is a classical algorithm that runs on the ___ rather than the GPU.

exercises.prompt: Which operation represents Selective Search grouping?
exercises.explain: Grouping pixels based on textures represents the classical heuristic-based Selective Search algorithm.

options.text: Merging adjacent pixels that share a similar brown wood texture into a candidate table region
Multiplying weights by gradients using Adam optimizer
Running max pooling with a stride of 2

exercises.prompt: You are building a classic object detector. Why does Selective Search evaluate color, texture, and size together rather than color alone?

options.text: A multicolored object (like a person wearing a blue shirt and red pants) would get split into separate regions if we only grouped by color.
Color is too slow to calculate on the CPU.
Using more cues reduces the number of classes.
To make the algorithm compatible with GPUs.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Objects often contain multiple colors, so combining texture and size cues helps the algorithm merge these disparate sections into a single object proposal. Next: let's examine this hierarchical merging process in detail!
